primary_diagnosis.name: Decompensated cor pulmonale (right-sided heart failure) secondary to end-stage COPD, with systemic venous congestion

pathophysiology_summary: Chronic alveolar hypoxia from severe COPD drives hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction and vascular remodelling, raising pulmonary vascular resistance and pulmonary artery pressure. The right ventricle hypertrophies and then dilates and fails against the chronic pressure load (cor pulmonale). Right ventricular failure raises systemic venous pressure, producing elevated JVP, tender hepatic congestion, ascites and dependent peripheral oedema. The decompensation here is precipitated by worsening hypoxaemia (compounded by home-oxygen non-compliance), which increases pulmonary vascular resistance and tips a compensated RV into overt failure. Correcting hypoxaemia with controlled oxygen lowers pulmonary vascular resistance and, with cautious diuresis, relieves the congestion; over-aggressive high-flow oxygen risks CO2 retention in a chronic CO2-retaining COPD patient, and over-diuresis risks pre-renal impairment.

differential.diagnosis: decompensated cor pulmonale / right heart failure
differential.evidence_basis: New bilateral oedema, abdominal distension and worsening dyspnoea in a patient with severe chronic lung disease points to right-sided congestive failure.
Worsening breathlessness in known COPD — but the absence of fever/purulent sputum and the prominent systemic oedema/ascites are not explained by an infective exacerbation alone.
Also causes oedema and dyspnoea; must be considered but the picture is dominated by systemic venous congestion rather than orthopnoea/pulmonary oedema.

differential.diagnosis: pulmonary embolism (subacute, with right heart strain)
differential.evidence_basis: Subacute PE can precipitate acute-on-chronic right heart strain and hypoxaemia; cannot be excluded remotely and warrants in-person assessment with ECG/imaging.

differential.diagnosis: decompensated cor pulmonale / right heart failure
differential.evidence_basis: Systemic congestion (bilateral oedema to thighs, ascites, tender RUQ) plus resting hypoxaemia and oxygen non-compliance fit right heart decompensation from COPD.

Ascites and tender hepatomegaly could suggest liver disease, but here they are congestive — accompanied by peripheral oedema and dyspnoea; distinguishing requires examination (elevated JVP) and investigation.
Oedema is symmetrical and bilateral and accompanied by ascites and systemic congestion — a central cause, not a unilateral venous thrombosis.

differential.diagnosis: decompensated cor pulmonale (right heart failure) secondary to severe COPD
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed in person by the clinician tetrad (RV heave, elevated JVP with v wave, loud P2, right-sided S3) plus congestive hepatomegaly/ascites and resting hypoxaemia; echocardiographic RV hypertrophy pending. This confirmation is NOT achievable via telehealth.
Worsening hypoxaemia contributes to the decompensation even without an infective flare.

red_flags.description: Resting hypoxaemia (home oximeter ~89% on room air) with visible central/peripheral cyanosis and accessory-muscle use
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals significant respiratory compromise and RV decompensation; a telehealth service cannot titrate oxygen or exclude acute deterioration, so this mandates same-day in-person assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to hypercapnic respiratory failure and cardiac collapse if managed at home without escalation.

red_flags.description: New rapidly progressive systemic venous congestion — bilateral oedema to the thighs, ascites, tender hepatomegaly
red_flags.clinical_significance: Marks overt right ventricular failure requiring cautious diuresis, electrolyte/renal monitoring and confirmatory imaging — none deliverable remotely.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Ongoing congestion, hepatic and renal compromise, and avoidable emergency admission.

red_flags.description: Warning signs that would convert this to an immediate 000 call — worsening confusion, extreme drowsiness, breathlessness despite oxygen, or inability to speak in sentences
red_flags.clinical_significance: These indicate CO2 narcosis / decompensated respiratory failure; currently absent (patient alert, speaking in sentences), which sets the floor at urgent in-person assessment rather than an immediate ambulance — but they must be safety-netted.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If they appear and are not acted on, hypercapnic coma and respiratory arrest.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing the worsening breathlessness to a simple infective COPD flare and reaching for antibiotics/oral steroids, missing the new right heart failure — there is no fever or purulent sputum to support infection.
Reading the leg swelling as a DVT and anticoagulating, when the oedema is bilateral and symmetric with ascites and systemic congestion — a central cause.
Taking the ascites and tender hepatomegaly at face value as primary liver disease, missing that they are congestive from right heart failure.
Correcting the low SpO2 with high-flow oxygen in a chronic CO2-retaining COPD patient, precipitating hypercapnic respiratory failure; controlled low-flow oxygen to a target range is required.

Mirroring the in-person clinician's home management (oral furosemide + oxygen + 48h GP review) without recognising that telehealth cannot examine, measure, dose or monitor — so the honest action is escalation to same-day in-person/ED assessment.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Elevated jugular venous pressure (6 cm above the sternal angle with a prominent v wave)
Palpable right ventricular heave at the left sternal border
Loud pulmonary component of the second heart sound (P2) and a right-sided S3
Shifting dullness / ascites and tender hepatomegaly on abdominal examination
Graded 3+ pitting oedema to the mid-thighs
Auscultated diffuse expiratory wheeze and prolonged expiratory phase
Exact blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate and a clinically validated SpO2
Echocardiographic right ventricular hypertrophy/strain, ECG, and electrolyte/renal bloods
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise decompensated right heart failure (cor pulmonale) on a background of severe COPD and escalate to a same-day in-person/ED assessment (T4) for examination, controlled oxygen titration, cautious diuresis with electrolyte/renal monitoring, ECG and echocardiography. Reinforce prescribed home oxygen use in the meantime and safety-net the 000 triggers.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Moderate — the syndrome (right heart failure) is recognisable from the pattern, but the specific cor pulmonale confirmation and the exclusion of dangerous mimics (subacute PE, biventricular failure) require in-person exam, ECG, bloods and echo. The dominant risk is UNDER-action: managing at home like the in-person clinician did, or mislabelling it as a simple COPD flare.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular — chronic (right-sided) heart failure
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Cautious loop-diuretic therapy for congestion with electrolyte and renal monitoring; treat the underlying cause and precipitant.

guidelines_referenced.reference: GOLD Report — COPD and cor pulmonale
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Controlled long-term oxygen therapy for chronic hypoxaemia to reduce pulmonary vascular resistance; avoid uncontrolled high-flow oxygen in CO2 retainers.
